sections.heading: Beyond intuition
sections.body: We've been describing Big O loosely — 'how time grows'. That's fine for daily use, but Big O has a precise mathematical definition. Knowing it lets you reason about borderline cases and answer questions like *'is O(n²+ n) really the same as O(n²)?'*

sections.heading: The definition
sections.body:
**f(n) = O(g(n))** if there exist positive constants **c** and **n₀** such that:

`0 ≤ f(n) ≤ c · g(n)` for all **n ≥ n₀**.

In plain English: from some input size onwards, `f(n)` never grows faster than a constant multiple of `g(n)`. The constant `c` lets us ignore small wobbles. The starting point `n₀` lets us ignore weird behavior at small inputs.

sections.title: Two cars on a long highway
sections.body: Car A might be a bit faster off the line, but for the rest of the trip Car B keeps up — never going more than 2× Car A's speed. Then we say Car B is O(Car A). The first few seconds don't matter; what matters is the long-run behavior.

sections.caption: Visualizing the definition: c·g(n) eventually bounds f(n)

steps.text: We want to show that n² + 1000n grows no faster than n². It feels like the 1000n term should matter, but it doesn't for large n.
steps.code:
f(n) = n² + 1000n
We want: f(n) ≤ c · n²  for n ≥ n₀

steps.text: Pick c = 2. We need n² + 1000n ≤ 2n², which simplifies to 1000n ≤ n², or n ≥ 1000.

steps.highlight: For any n ≥ 1000, f(n) ≤ 2n². So f(n) = O(n²).

steps.text: Notice how big n had to get before the n² term dominated. This is why Big O is about asymptotic behavior — what happens 'eventually'.

sections.heading: Why we drop constants and lower-order terms
sections.body:
Once you have the definition, the simplifications fall out for free:

* **Drop constants**: O(5n) = O(n). Pick c' = 5c.
* **Drop lower-order terms**: O(n² + n) = O(n²). For large n, n is dwarfed by n², and we can absorb it into a bigger c.
* **Drop logs vs constants**: O(log n) ≪ O(n) because log grows arbitrarily slowly.

The definition gives you license to be sloppy about details that vanish at scale.

questions.question: Which constants c and n₀ prove 2n + 100 = O(n)?
questions.explanation: We need 2n + 100 ≤ 3n, which gives 100 ≤ n. So c = 3, n₀ = 100 works.

questions.question: Is O(n² + 1000) the same as O(n²)?
options: Yes, constants and lower-order terms drop
Only if we add log n to both sides
questions.explanation: The definition lets us absorb any constant into a bigger c. The 1000 vanishes.

questions.question: If f(n) = O(g(n)), it means f grows no faster than a constant times g — for all sufficiently large ___.
questions.explanation: Big O is an asymptotic statement: it holds for all n ≥ some threshold n₀.

questions.question: Is f(n) = n a valid bound for f(n) = log n? (i.e. is log n = O(n)?)
options: Yes, log grows slower than linear
questions.explanation: log n grows much slower than n, so n is an upper bound. log n = O(n) is correct but loose.
